threats.title: Emergency Mode Fails to Unblock ETH Withdrawals Post-Conversion
threats.hypothesis_description: The emergency mode is designed to allow users to withdraw assets at any time, but a hardcoded state check permanently blocks ETH withdrawals after conversion, defeating the emergency mechanism.
threats.investigation_rationale: Emergency mechanisms must reliably bypass standard state locks. If the lpETH contract fails or is malicious, the owner will activate emergency mode to rescue funds. However, ETH depositors will remain permanently locked out due to the UseClaimInstead check.

proof_of_signal.pattern: if (block.timestamp >= startClaimDate) revert UseClaimInstead(); executes outside the emergencyMode bypass

preconditions: convertAllETH has been called (startClaimDate is set)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: withdraw() checks startClaimDate for ETH tokens regardless of emergencyMode status
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Admin relies on emergencyMode to rescue funds if lpETH integration fails
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time after convertAllETH is executed

operational_questions: Can ETH depositors recover funds if the lpETH contract is compromised post-conversion?
Does emergencyMode function as a true failsafe for all assets?
validation_requirements: Test withdraw(ETH) after convertAllETH with emergencyMode = true
Verify that recoverERC20 cannot rescue lpETH

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner calls convertAllETH, transitioning protocol to claim phase
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: lpETH contract suffers a critical failure or is discovered to be malicious
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Owner enables emergencyMode to allow users to withdraw their original deposits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: ETH depositors call withdraw(ETH) but transaction reverts with UseClaimInstead

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Admin loses ability to rescue ETH depositor funds
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Permanent loss of all ETH deposits if lpETH claim path is broken
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Impossible without a contract upgrade (which is not supported)

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Failed withdraw transactions during emergency mode
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users unable to recover funds despite emergency mode activation

threats.title: Zero-Cost Drain of Contract ETH via LRT Claim Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: An attacker can drain all contract ETH by executing a zero-amount LRT claim that captures the entire contract balance instead of the swap delta.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _claim function uses address(this).balance to determine how much lpETH to mint, rather than measuring the ETH received from the 0x swap. A 0% claim executes a zero-value swap but mints lpETH equal to the entire contract ETH balance.
preconditions: Contract holds residual ETH (e.g., from receive() or selfdestruct)
Attacker has a non-zero balance of any allowed LRT token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: claimedAmount uses absolute balance instead of post-swap delta
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any time residual ETH exists in the contract post-conversion

operational_questions: Does the contract accept arbitrary ETH transfers post-conversion?
Can a user execute a claim with _percentage = 0?
validation_requirements: Send 1 ETH to the contract via receive() after convertAllETH
Call claim() with _percentage = 0 for an LRT token
Verify attacker receives 1 lpETH while their LRT balance remains unchanged

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor contract for residual ETH accumulation post-conversion
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Call claim() or claimAndStake() with _percentage = 0 and valid 0-amount 0x calldata
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Contract executes 0-amount swap, leaving attacker's LRT balance unchanged
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Contract deposits its entire ETH balance into lpETH and credits it to the attacker

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of all unaccounted ETH in the contract
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: High - stolen funds are minted as valid lpETH

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: claim() calls with _percentage = 0 or resulting in 0 tokens swapped
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: lpETH minted exceeds the expected swap value of the LRT tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Repeated claims by the same address without reducing their LRT balance

threats.title: 0x Calldata Validation Bypass via Non-Standard ABI Encoding
threats.hypothesis_description: Hardcoded assembly offsets in calldata decoding allow attackers to bypass token validation and execute arbitrary 0x swaps.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _decodeUniswapV3Data function assumes the bytes payload is located at offset 128. An attacker can craft non-standard ABI encoding where the real payload is at a different offset, placing fake data at offset 128 to pass validation while the 0x proxy executes the real payload.

proof_of_signal.pattern: inputToken := shr(96, calldataload(add(p, 128))) uses hardcoded offset for dynamic bytes array

preconditions: Attacker is claiming an LRT token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Assembly decoding of dynamic bytes arrays without reading the actual offset pointer

operational_questions: Can the 0x exchange proxy process calldata with non-standard bytes offsets?
Does the validation bypass allow attackers to extract value from the protocol?
validation_requirements: Construct calldata with bytes offset pointing to actual swap data
Place fake inputToken/outputToken at offset 128 to pass _validateData
Verify 0x proxy executes the swap using the actual swap data

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Craft a malicious UniswapV3 swap path that routes through an attacker-controlled pool
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Encode the 0x calldata with a non-standard bytes offset (e.g., 256)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Place the expected inputToken and WETH addresses at offset 128 to satisfy _validateData
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Submit the claim; validation passes, but 0x executes the malicious path, extracting value
preliminary_attack_methodology.coordination_requirements: Requires setting up a malicious Uniswap V3 pool

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Attackers can extract value from their own swaps, potentially leaving the contract with less ETH than expected

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: High - requires deep understanding of ABI encoding and 0x proxy mechanics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Claims executing swaps through unknown or low-liquidity pools
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Mismatch between validated tokens and actual swapped tokens
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Complex calldata structures with padding or unusual offsets

threats.title: Arbitrary Execution via Unvalidated TransformERC20 Transformations
threats.hypothesis_description: Lack of validation on TransformERC20 transformations allows arbitrary execution and potential fund redirection during claims.
threats.investigation_rationale: When using the TransformERC20 exchange type, _validateData only checks the input/output tokens and amount. It does not validate the transformations array, which can contain arbitrary instructions for the 0x proxy, potentially allowing redirection of funds or reentrancy.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Only reads inputToken, outputToken, and amount; ignores transformations array

preconditions: Attacker is claiming an LRT token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Delegating execution to a flexible proxy without validating the execution payload

operational_questions: What transformations are permitted by the connected 0x proxy?
Can a transformation redirect the output ETH to an attacker address instead of the contract?
validation_requirements: Test a claim with a transformation that sends funds to a third party

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Construct a TransformERC20 payload with a custom transformation
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: The transformation swaps the LRT to ETH but transfers the ETH to the attacker
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit the claim; validation passes because input/output tokens match
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: 0x proxy executes the transformation, sending ETH to the attacker while the contract receives nothing

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of swap proceeds or potential exploitation of contract approvals

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: High - requires understanding of 0x TransformERC20 mechanics

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: TransformERC20 calls with unusual transformation sequences
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Claims that result in 0 ETH being returned to the contract

threats.title: Persistent DoS of LRT Claims via Dusting
threats.hypothesis_description: Attackers can persistently DoS user claims by dusting their accounts via lockFor, causing strict calldata amount validation to fail.
threats.investigation_rationale: The claim function strictly validates that the 0x calldata inputTokenAmount exactly matches the calculated userClaim (based on userStake). Because anyone can increase a victim's userStake using lockFor, an attacker can invalidate the victim's pre-signed/pre-calculated 0x calldata by dusting their account.
preconditions: Victim is attempting to claim an LRT token
loopActivation has not passed (deposits still open)

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Strict equality check on a value derived from a manipulable state variable

operational_questions: Can an attacker reliably dust a victim before their claim transaction is confirmed?
Does the strict equality check prevent the claim from executing?
validation_requirements: Simulate a victim generating 0x calldata for their current balance
Execute lockFor with 1 wei to the victim
Attempt to execute the victim's claim transaction and verify it reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Monitor for users preparing to claim LRTs
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Periodically call lockFor with 1 wei of the target LRT to the victim's address
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Victim's userStake increases, altering the calculated userClaim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Victim's claim transaction reverts due to WrongDataAmount

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users are unable to claim their LRTs, causing frustration and potential loss of favorable swap rates
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low - victim must recalculate and submit quickly

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Frequent lockFor calls with dust amounts
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: High rate of reverted claim transactions with WrongDataAmount

threats.title: Residual Token Approvals Expose User Funds to 0x Proxy
threats.hypothesis_description: Residual token approvals to the 0x exchange proxy can be exploited by subsequent claims if the proxy does not consume the full allowance.
threats.investigation_rationale: The _fillQuote function approves the exchangeProxy for the exact swap amount but does not reset the approval to zero after the call. If the 0x proxy executes a partial fill or a custom transformation that doesn't consume the full allowance, the residual approval remains. A subsequent attacker could craft a claim that leverages this leftover allowance.
preconditions: A user executes a claim where the 0x proxy does not consume the full approved amount
Attacker executes a subsequent claim using the same token

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Leaving residual ERC20 approvals to a complex external proxy

operational_questions: Under what conditions does the 0x proxy leave residual allowances?
Can an attacker craft a 0x payload that consumes the contract's residual allowance for their own benefit?
validation_requirements: Test a swap that intentionally consumes less than the approved amount
Attempt to consume the residual allowance in a subsequent transaction

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Wait for a user to execute a claim that leaves a residual allowance (e.g., via specific slippage settings)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Craft a malicious 0x payload that attempts to transfer more tokens than the attacker's own userClaim
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Submit the claim; the 0x proxy consumes the attacker's tokens plus the residual allowance
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Attacker receives more ETH/lpETH than they are entitled to

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Theft of user tokens held in the contract

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Non-zero allowances to the exchangeProxy after claim execution
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Contract token balance drops below the sum of user balances

threats.title: Irreversible Protocol Brick via Unvalidated Loop Addresses
threats.hypothesis_description: Lack of address validation in setLoopAddresses allows the owner to permanently brick the protocol, locking all ETH.
threats.investigation_rationale: The setLoopAddresses function can only be called once because it resets loopActivation to block.timestamp. It lacks zero-address or contract-code checks. If the owner makes a mistake, the addresses are permanently set to invalid values, causing convertAllETH to revert and permanently locking all ETH deposits.
threats.config_evidence: PrelaunchPoints.sol: setLoopAddresses function lacks validation and is a one-time call.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No address(0) checks; loopActivation = uint32(block.timestamp) prevents re-calling

preconditions: Owner calls setLoopAddresses with incorrect or zero addresses

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: One-time critical configuration function lacks basic input validation
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Protocol survival depends entirely on flawless admin execution

operational_questions: Is there any recovery mechanism if setLoopAddresses is called incorrectly?
Does emergencyMode allow ETH recovery if convertAllETH cannot be called?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call convertAllETH and verify it reverts

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Owner accidentally or maliciously calls setLoopAddresses with invalid addresses
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: loopActivation is updated, preventing any correction
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: TIMELOCK expires, owner attempts to call convertAllETH but it reverts
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Protocol is permanently bricked; ETH cannot be converted or withdrawn

potential_operational_impact.administrative_compromise: Admin loses ability to activate protocol
potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Total loss of all locked ETH
